course_title: Bioarchaeology: Human remains and ancient disease

learning_outcomes.description: Identify and describe characteristics of human skeletal anatomy (including naming and siding skeletal elements, sexual dimorphism, ancestry, stature calculation, and bone pathology in the human skeleton).
Describe and explain the relationship between pathology and major developments in the human past
Describe and explain the scientific analysis techniques used to study ancient human bones and soft tissues
Describe cultural approaches to death seen in the archaeological record
Describe and explain the factors that act to preserve or decay human remains in archaeological environments
Describe and explain the correct method of skeletal excavation, recording, transportation and storage
Explain the ethical issues arising from the excavation and analysis of human remains

assessments.due_date:
Week 3 13/08/2026

Week 7 10/09/2026

Week 11 15/10/2026

Completed in-class Practical Sessions Week 3, Week 7, Week 11.
assessments.task_description:
Three in class quizzes (1 hour) will be delivered over the semester. The quizzes will be conducted during laboratory time. Quizzes are timed assessments that require answering short answer/multiple choice questions and the identification of skeletal elements, naming of landmarks, and identification of modifications etc. They will test cumulative knowledge gained through the lectures and practical sessions in the course. Content from all weeks prior to the test may be included.

Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You must submit your assessment at the end of class to the course coordinator / assessment invigilator.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
Students will research and produce a report on palaeopathology using a number of case studies provided in class. The task will be mainly focussed on 3D scans and x-rays from an important archaeological site and you will need to undertake a differential diagnosis to determine the correct pathology. An extensive guidance document containing an example report format is provided on the ARCS2070 Blackboard site in the 'Assessment' section to give students guidance as to the report style and level of detail required for this assessment.

Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction to Bioarchaeology

Every skeleton tells a story. This introductory lecture provides an overview of the course and introduces bioarchaeology as the study of human remains from archaeological sites. Students will explore how bioarchaeologists use skeletal evidence to reconstruct the lives, deaths and histories of past peoples, while considering the scientific, archaeological and ethical foundations of the discipline.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L07
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction to Bioarchaeology Every skeleton tells a story. Students will explore how bioarchaeologists use skeletal evidence to reconstruct the lives, deaths and histories of past peoples, while considering the scientific, archaeological and ethical foundations of the discipline.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction to Terminology and Concepts

Introduction to terminology and concepts used in the study of skeletal remains and disease. Introduction to correct handling of specimens.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction to Terminology and Concepts Introduction to terminology and concepts used in the study of skeletal remains and disease. Introduction to correct handling of specimens.

learning_activities.topic:
Current Debates and Theory in Bioarchaeology

Explore the ideas shaping modern bioarchaeology. This lecture examines the major theoretical approaches and current debates in the discipline, including the osteological paradox, the bioarchaeology of care, osteobiography, identity, embodiment, and the ethical interpretation of human remains.

Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L05, L07
learning_activities.topic_description: Current Debates and Theory in Bioarchaeology Explore the ideas shaping modern bioarchaeology. This lecture examines the major theoretical approaches and current debates in the discipline, including the osteological paradox, the bioarchaeology of care, osteobiography, identity, embodiment, and the ethical interpretation of human remains.

learning_activities.topic:
Techniques in Bioarchaeology

Ancient remains have remarkable stories to tell. This lecture introduces the powerful scientific techniques that allow archaeologists to reconstruct the lives of people who lived hundreds or even thousands of years ago. Through ancient DNA, proteomics, isotope analysis, dental macrowear and histology, students will learn how researchers investigate ancestry, migration, diet, health, disease and childhood development to bring the past to life.

Learning outcomes: L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Techniques in Bioarchaeology Ancient remains have remarkable stories to tell. learning_activities.topic:
Ethics in Bioarchaeology

In the lecture ethics in bioarchaeology we will explore how bioarchaeological research can be conducted in ways that are respectful, culturally appropriate and beneficial to descendant communities. Focusing on community bioarchaeology, Indigenous partnerships, repatriation and collaborative research, the course considers how ethical practice can foster trust, accountability and meaningful relationships while advancing our understanding of the human past.

Learning outcomes: L06, L07

learning_activities.topic:
Mortuary Archaeology

Mortuary archaeology explores the relationship between the living and the dead in past societies. Using archaeological, osteological and biomolecular evidence, it examines funerary practices, mortuary landscapes and human remains to investigate identity, kinship, mobility, health, inequality, ritual and cultural change. We will look at examples internationally but also have a particular focus on mortuary archaeology in Australia.

Learning outcomes: L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Mortuary Archaeology Mortuary archaeology explores the relationship between the living and the dead in past societies. Using archaeological, osteological and biomolecular evidence, it examines funerary practices, mortuary landscapes and human remains to investigate identity, kinship, mobility, health, inequality, ritual and cultural change. We will look at examples internationally but also have a particular focus on mortuary archaeology in Australia.

learning_activities.topic:
Taphonomy and Burial Excavation

Every skeleton has survived a remarkable journey. This lecture explores the natural processes that determine whether human remains are preserved, altered or lost in the archaeological record. Students will examine the science of taphonomy and learn the professional standards for the excavation, documentation, recovery, transport and curation of human remains, ensuring that invaluable archaeological evidence is preserved for future research.

Learning outcomes: L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Palaeodemography: Reconstructing Ancient Populations

How can archaeologists estimate the size, structure, health, and life expectancy of people who lived thousands of years ago? This lecture introduces the principles of palaeodemography, exploring how skeletal remains are used to reconstruct population age structure, mortality, fertility, and patterns of survival, while considering the strengths, limitations, and biases of archaeological evidence.

Learning outcomes: L01
learning_activities.topic_description: Palaeodemography: Reconstructing Ancient Populations How can archaeologists estimate the size, structure, health, and life expectancy of people who lived thousands of years ago? learning_activities.topic:
Metabolic Disease and Neoplasia in Bioarchaeology

Why did some diseases become more common as people adopted farming and built cities? This lecture examines the skeletal evidence for nutritional deficiencies, hormonal disorders, and cancer, revealing how diet, environment, urbanisation, and social inequality influenced health across human history.

Learning outcomes: L01
learning_activities.topic_description: Metabolic Disease and Neoplasia in Bioarchaeology Why did some diseases become more common as people adopted farming and built cities? learning_activities.topic:
Congenital and Developmental Disorders in Bioarchaeology

This lecture examines the identification and interpretation of congenital and developmental disorders in archaeological skeletal remains. Students will also explore evidence for obstetric complications and difficult childbirth, and consider what these conditions reveal about health, survival, and care in past populations.

Learning outcomes: L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Congenital and Developmental Disorders in Bioarchaeology This lecture examines the identification and interpretation of congenital and developmental disorders in archaeological skeletal remains. Students will also explore evidence for obstetric complications and difficult childbirth, and consider what these conditions reveal about health, survival, and care in past populations.

learning_activities.topic:
Infectious Disease in Bioarchaeology

Why did infectious diseases become some of humanity's greatest killers? This lecture explores the archaeological evidence for bacterial and viral disease and examines how the transition to farming, animal domestication, urbanisation, colonialism, and warfare transformed patterns of health and disease throughout human history.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Infectious Disease in Bioarchaeology Why did infectious diseases become some of humanity's greatest killers? This lecture explores the archaeological evidence for bacterial and viral disease and examines how the transition to farming, animal domestication, urbanisation, colonialism, and warfare transformed patterns of health and disease throughout human history.

learning_activities.topic:
Invisible Invaders: Parasites and the Human Past

This lecture explores the archaeological and bioarchaeological evidence for parasitic disease and how human behaviour created new environments for infection. It examines the impact of sedentism, agriculture, animal domestication, urbanisation, colonial expansion, and conflict on the spread and evolution of parasitic diseases through time.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02

learning_activities.topic:
Bodies Under Pressure: Work, Ageing, and Inequality in the Human Past

Every skeleton carries a record of how a person lived and worked. This lecture investigates occupational and degenerative diseases in archaeological populations and explores how major social transformations—from the rise of agriculture to industrialisation—reshaped patterns of bodily stress and ageing. A guest lecture introduces how histology allows us to see disease and trauma at the microscopic level, revealing hidden details of life histories.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02

learning_activities.topic:
Beyond Bone: Mummies and Exceptional Soft Tissue Preservation

How do human tissues survive for thousands of years? This lecture explores the extraordinary preservation of soft tissues in the archaeological record, including mummified remains and bog bodies. Students will examine the environmental and chemical conditions that allow soft tissue survival and explore the scientific techniques used to analyse these rare and remarkable remains.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L05, L06
